title: Propositional Logic - Standard Level Question Paper

questions.answer: A proposition that is true for all possible truth assignments of its variables.
If P → Q and P are true, then Q is true.

questions.q: What is the negation of P → Q?
When is a compound proposition called a contradiction?
questions.answer: When it is false for all truth assignments of its variables.

questions.q: Write the commutative law for conjunction.
Differentiate between contingency and contradiction with an example each.
questions.answer: Contingency: true for some assignments, false for others (e.g., P ∧ Q). Contradiction: false for all assignments (e.g., P ∧ ¬P).

questions.q: Write the truth table for exclusive OR (XOR).
State De Morgan’s laws in propositional logic.
What is the difference between logical equivalence and implication?
questions.answer: Equivalence (↔) means both sides have same truth value; implication (→) means if antecedent true then consequent true, but not vice versa.

questions.q: Prove that P → Q ≡ ¬P ∨ Q using truth table (show only the final columns).
questions.answer:
P Q | P→Q | ¬P∨Q
0 0 | 1   | 1
0 1 | 1   | 1
1 0 | 0   | 0
1 1 | 1   | 1
All columns match, hence equivalent.

questions.q: Using logical equivalence laws, simplify: (P → Q) ∧ (P → ¬Q).
questions.explanation: The expression reduces to ¬P, meaning if P implies both Q and ¬Q, then P must be false.

questions.q: Check whether the following argument is valid using truth table: P → Q, ¬Q ⊢ ¬P.
questions.explanation: Only row where both premises are true is row 1, and there ¬P is true. Hence argument is valid (Modus Tollens).

questions.q: Prove the hypothetical syllogism: (P → Q) ∧ (Q → R) ⇒ (P → R) using logical laws.
questions.explanation: Hypothetical syllogism is a fundamental inference rule: if P implies Q and Q implies R, then P implies R.

questions.q: Construct a truth table for the proposition: (P ∨ Q) → (P ∧ Q). Is it a tautology, contradiction, or contingency?
questions.answer:
P Q | P∨Q | P∧Q | (P∨Q)→(P∧Q)
0 0 | 0   | 0   | 1
0 1 | 1   | 0   | 0
1 0 | 1   | 0   | 0
1 1 | 1   | 1   | 1
It is a contingency (neither all true nor all false).

questions.q:
Using the rules of inference (Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Hypothetical Syllogism, Disjunctive Syllogism, Addition, Simplification, Conjunction, Resolution), prove the following argument is valid:

Premises:
1. P → (Q ∨ R)
2. ¬Q
3. ¬R
Conclusion: ¬P

Show each step with the rule used.
questions.explanation: By combining ¬Q and ¬R we get ¬(Q∨R). Since P implies (Q∨R), the negation of the consequent forces the negation of the antecedent.
